name: Barbell Good Mornings
instructions: Set the height of the pins in a squat rack so that the barbell can lay across your shoulders comfortably. Next, set the safety bars at the desired height (typically waist high) so that if you fail on a rep, they will take the weight and you will be safe. Load up the barbell with the correct amount of weight on it and you are now ready to go.

Step underneath the bar and take the weight across your shoulders. Maintain a straight back and a small bend at your knees when unracking the bar. Press through your legs to lift the bar up and take a small step backwards into the middle of the rack.
Adopt a shoulder width stance, maintain a straight back throughout and keep your head up. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and begin to lean forward from your waist. Push your hips and glutes backward as you do so until your torso is just off being parallel to the floor. Your hamstrings should be under tension at this point.

After a brief pause, exhale and return the barbell to the starting position along the same path, thrusting your hips forward, squeezing your glutes as you do so. Stand to attention with your legs fully extended, back straight and shoulders relaxed. (Remember to keep your back straight throughout the whole movement).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you suffer from any back injuries, this may not be the most suitable exercise for you.
Variations: Barbell Hip Thrusts or Stiff Leg Dumbbell Deadlifts.

Stand to attention with your legs fully extended, back straight and shoulders relaxed. (Remember to keep your back straight throughout the whole movement).

name: Barbell Front Squats Clean Grip
instructions: To begin, first set the bar in a rack slightly below shoulder level.  Rest the bar on top of the deltoids, pushing into the clavicles,  and lightly touching the throat. Your hands should be in a clean grip, touching the bar only with your fingers to help keep it in position.

Lift the bar off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.  Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out.  Keep your head and elbows up at all times. This will be your starting position.

Bend at the knees, sitting down between your legs.  Continue down until your hamstrings are on your calves. Keep your knees aligned with your feet by consciously using your abductors to push your knees out as you squat.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor mainly with the heel or middle of your foot as you straighten the legs again and return to the starting position.

Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor mainly with the heel or middle of your foot as you straighten the legs again and return to the starting position.

name: Barbell Front Squats To A Bench Clean Grip
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes.  To begin, first set a flat bench behind you and set the bar on a rack that best matches your height.  Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, bring your arms up under the bar while keeping the elbows high and the upper arm slightly above parallel to the floor.  Rest the bar on top of the deltoids and cross your arms while grasping the bar for total control.
Lift the bar off the rack by first pushing with your legs and at the same time straightening your torso.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out.  Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back.  This will be your starting position.  (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances described in the foot positioning section).

Begin to slowly lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up.  Continue down until you touch the bench with your glutes.  Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.  Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front.  If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor mainly with the heel of your foot as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Caution: This is not an exercise to be taken lightly.  If you have back issues, substitute it with the dumbbell squat variation or a leg press instead.  If you have a healthy back, ensure perfect form and never slouch the back forward as this can cause back injury. Be cautious as well with the weight used; in case of doubt, use less weight rather than more.  The front squat is a very safe exercise but only if performed properly.  This version of the squat is better suited for advanced athletes.
Variations: As previously mentioned, there are various stances that can be used depending on what you want to emphasize.

name: Barbell Front Squats Cross Grip
instructions: Set the height of the pins in a squat rack so that the barbell can lay across your shoulders comfortably. Next, set the safety bars at the desired height (typically waist high) so that if you fail on a rep, they will take the weight and you will be safe. Load up the barbell with the correct amount of weight on it and you are now ready to go.

Step underneath the bar and take the weight across the front of your shoulders. You can either relax your wrists and place then under the bar to secure its position or you can cross your arms over and lift your elbows, which ever is most comfortable for yourself. Maintain a straight back and a small bend at your knees when unracking the bar. Press through your legs to lift the bar up and take a small step backwards into the middle of the rack.
Adopt a shoulder width stance with your toes slightly pointing outward. Maintain a straight back throughout the movement and keep your head up. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and begin to descend by bending at your knees and hips. Your weight should be put predominantly through your heels as you sit back into the movement until your thighs are either parallel or just past to the floor.

After a brief pause, exhale and return the bar to the starting position along the same path, push through your heels with the force being driven through your quads to do so. Stand to attention with your legs fully extended, back straight and shoulders relaxed.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is a very complex movement and will require a spotter. If you do not have a spotter, be cautious with your weights. If you suffer from back issues, this exercise may not be suitable.
Variations: Barbell Squats or Kettlebell Squats.

name: Zercher Squat
instructions: This exercise is best performed inside a squat rack for safety purposes.  To begin, first set the bar on a rack that best matches your height.  The correct height should be anywhere above the waist but below the chest. Once the correct height is chosen and the bar is loaded, lock your hands together and place the bar on top of your arms in between the forearm and upper arm.
Lift the bar up so that it is resting on top of your forearms.  If you are holding the bar properly, it should look as if you have your arms crossed but with a bar running across them.

Step away from the rack and position your legs using a shoulder width medium stance with the toes slightly pointed out.  Keep your head up at all times as looking down will get you off balance and also maintain a straight back.  This will be your starting position.  (Note: For the purposes of this discussion we will use the medium stance described above which targets overall development; however you can choose any of the three stances discussed in the foot stances section).

Begin to lower the bar by bending the knees as you maintain a straight posture with the head up.  Continue down until the angle between the upper leg and the calves becomes slightly less than 90-degrees (which is the point in which the upper legs are below parallel to the floor).  Inhale as you perform this portion of the movement.  Tip: If you performed the exercise correctly, the front of the knees should make an imaginary straight line with the toes that is perpendicular to the front.  If your knees are past that imaginary line (if they are past your toes) then you are placing undue stress on the knee and the exercise has been performed incorrectly.
Begin to raise the bar as you exhale by pushing the floor with the ball of your foot mainly as you straighten the legs again and go back to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

Caution: This exercise is not to be taken lightly as you are applying a lot of pressure to your arms when lifting the barbell.  It is always a good idea to have a spotter as they can help you keep proper form.  Also, make sure that your back is kept straight as bending it can cause an injury.

name: Standing Narrow Grip EZ Bar Overhead Tricep Extensions
instructions: Grab a barbell with an overhand, shoulder width grip and press towards the ceiling until your elbows are locked out. Adopt a standing, shoulder width stance with your back straight, barbell directly above your head. Tip: Keep your elbows as close together as you can throughout to initiate maximum tricep activity. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and descend the barbell on a semi-circular motion down towards the back of your head. Keep the upper arms still and control this movement through bending your elbows and stretching your triceps. Remember to keep your elbows tucked in.
Pause for a brief movement once you have created an acute angle at your elbows. Then pull through your triceps to take the barbell back along the same path to your starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Overhead Tricep Extension (Rope On Cables) or Barbell Skullcrushers (Incline).

name: Frankenstein Squats
instructions: This drill teaches you the proper positioning of both the bar and your body during the clean and front squat.

Place the barbell on the front of the shoulders, releasing your grip and extending your arms out in front of you. The shoulders should be pushed forward to create a shelf, and the bar should be in contact with the throat. Ensure that you only move your shoulder blades forward; don't round the thoracic spine.

Squat by flexing the knees and hips, sitting in between your legs. Keep the torso upright, the arms up, and the shoulders forward, and the bar should stay in place. Go to the bottom of the squat, until your hamstrings contact your calves.
Return to the upright position by driving through the front of the heel and extending the knees and hips.

name: Barbell Floor Press With Chains
instructions: Adjust the j-hooks so they are at the appropriate height to rack the bar. For this exercise, drape the chains directly over the end of the bar, trying to keep the ends away from the plates.
Begin lying on the floor with your head near the end of a power rack. Keeping your shoulder blades pulled together, pull the bar off of the hooks.

Lower the bar towards the bottom of your chest or upper stomach, squeezing the bar and attempting to pull it apart as you do so. Ensure that you tuck your elbows throughout the movement. Lower the bar until your upper arm contacts the ground and pause, preventing any slamming or bouncing of the weight.
Press the bar back up as fast as you can, keeping the bar, your wrists, and elbows in line as you do so.

name: Palms Up Barbell Wrist Curls Over A Bench
instructions: Hold a barbell with both hands and your palms facing up; hands spaced about shoulder width.
Place your feet flat on the floor, at a distance that is slightly wider than shoulder width apart. This will be your starting position.
Lower the bar as far as possible by extending the fingers. Allowing the bar to roll down the hands, catch the bar with the final joint in the fingers.
Now curl bar up as high as possible by closing your hands while exhaling. Hold the contraction at the top.

name: Decline Narrow Grip EZ Bar Skullcrushers
instructions: Grab an EZ barbell and lay on a decline bench with the bar pressed straight up towards the ceiling. Adopt a pronated (overhand) grip with your elbows tucked in. This will be your starting position.

Inhale as you descend the barbell down towards the back of your head. Be sure to keep your elbows tucked in and stretch your triceps as you go. When you get to within an inch of the bench, pause for a second.
Then whilst exhaling, use your triceps to return the bar along the same path to the starting position. Your arms should be fully extended once again and pointing towards the ceiling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Decline Press Ups or Overhead Tricep Extensions Rope.

name: Narrow Grip EZ Bar Skullcrushers
instructions: Grab an EZ bar (overhand grip) and lay flat on a bench with the bar pressed straight up towards the ceiling. Keep your elbows as close together as you can to initiate maximum tricep activity. This will be your starting position.
Inhale as you descend the bar towards your forehead. Be sure to keep your upper arms stationary and your elbows tucked in. When you get to within an inch of your forehead pause for a second.
Then whilst exhaling, use your triceps to return the bar to the starting position. Your arms should be fully extended once again and pointing towards the ceiling.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Narrow Grip Barbell Bench Press or Tricep Rope Extensions.

name: Wide Grip EZ Bar Curls
instructions: Grab an EZ bar with a shoulder width, underhand grip (palms facing forward). Allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor and adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. This will be your starting position.
Whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the bar upward towards your shoulders by flexing your biceps. Exhale as you do so and remember, only your forearms should move.
Continue in this fashion until your biceps are fully flexed and the bar is at shoulder height.
Pause for a brief moment and then return the bar along the same path toward the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Standing Barbell Curls or Cable Curls.

en: biceps

Grab an EZ bar with a shoulder width, underhand grip (palms facing forward). Allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor and adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. This will be your starting position.
Whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the bar upward towards your shoulders by flexing your biceps. Exhale as you do so and remember, only your forearms should move.
Continue in this fashion until your biceps are fully flexed and the bar is at shoulder height.
Pause for a brief moment and then return the bar along the same path toward the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Standing Barbell Curls or Cable Curls.

name: Drop Snatch
instructions: This drill helps you learn the snatch. Begin by holding a light weight across the back of the shoulders. Your feet should be slightly wider than hip width apart with the feet turned out, the same position that you would perform a squat with.
Begin by dipping with the knees slightly, and popping back up to briefly unload the bar. Drive yourself underneath the bar, elevating it overhead as you descend into a full squat.
Return to a standing position.

name: Barbell Drag Curl
instructions: Grab a barbell with a supinated grip (palms facing forward) and get your elbows close to your torso and back. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, curl the bar up while keeping the elbows to the back as you "Drag" the bar up by keeping it in contact with your torso.  Tip: As you can see, you will not be keeping the elbows pinned to your sides, but instead you will be bringing them back.  Also, do not lift your shoulders.
Slowly go back to the starting position as you keep the bar in contact with the torso at all times.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: You can use dumbbells or the smith machine.

name: Smith Machine Decline Bench Press
instructions: Lay on a decline bench in the smith machine. Take a medium grip (a grip which creates a 90 degree angle at your elbows half way through the movement) and rotate your wrists so that you take the weight of the bar, at this point it should be directly above your chest. Keep your elbows locked at this point for safety. This will be your starting position. Tip: Make sure you set the height of the safety bars just in case you get in trouble.
Whilst inhaling, start to lower the bar by bending at your elbows until the bar gently touches your lower chest.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the bar back into the starting position by contracting your pectoral muscles.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When you are done, rotate your wrists so that the barbell is then securely racked up.
Caution: You should always have a spotter when attempting this movement. If a spotter is not available you should be conservative with the weight you are using.
Variations: Decline Barbell Bench Press or Decline Dumbbell Chest Press.

name: Decline Close-Grip Bench To Skull Crusher
instructions: Secure your legs at the end of the decline bench and slowly lay down on the bench.

Using a close grip (a grip that is slightly less than shoulder width), lift the bar from the rack and hold it straight over you with your arms locked and elbows in.  The arms should be perpendicular to the floor.  This will be your starting position. Tip: In order to protect your rotator cuff, it is best if you have a spotter help you lift the barbell off the rack.
Now lower the bar down to your lower chest as you breathe in.  Keep the elbows in as you perform this movement.
Using the triceps to push the bar back up, press it back to the starting position as you exhale.

As you breathe in and you keep the upper arms stationary, bring the bar down slowly by moving your forearms in a semicircular motion towards you until you feel the bar slightly touch your forehead.  Breathe in as you perform this portion of the movement.
Lift the bar back to the starting position by contracting the triceps and exhaling.
Repeat steps 3-6 until the recommended amount of repetitions is performed.
Variations: You can use an e-z bar or dumbbells to perform this movement.  You can also perform it on a flat bench as well.

name: Deadlift from a Deficit
instructions: Begin by having a platform or weight plates that you can stand on, usually 1-3 inches in height. Approach the bar so that it is centered over your feet. You feet should be about hip width apart. Bend at the hip to grip the bar at shoulder width, allowing your shoulder blades to protract. Typically, you would use an overhand grip or an over/under grip on heavier sets.

With your feet, and your grip set, take a big breath and then lower your hips and bend the knees until your shins contact the bar. Look forward with your head, keep your chest up and your back arched, and begin driving through the heels to move the weight upward. After the bar passes the knees, aggressively pull the bar back, pulling your shoulder blades together as you drive your hips forward into the bar.
Lower the bar by bending at the hips and guiding it to the floor.

name: Narrow Grip Barbell Preacher Curls
instructions: Grab a barbell with an underhand grip and take a seat on a preacher curl machine. Place your upper arms over the pad of the machine with your palms facing forward. Set the height of the seat so that your arm pits are snug on top of the pad and have your arms extended. This will be your starting position.

Exhale and contract your biceps to lift the barbell up towards your shoulders. Keep your triceps on the pad and squeeze your biceps until they are fully contracted and the barbell is at shoulder height.
After a brief pause, inhale and slowly lower the barbell along the same path back into its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Cable Curls or Two-Arm Dumbbell Preacher Curl.

name: Close-Grip Standing Barbell Curl
instructions: Grab a barbell with a narrow, underhand grip (palms facing forward), and allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor. Adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. This will be your starting position.
Whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the barbell upward toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps. Exhale as you do so and remember, only your forearms should move.
Continue in this fashion until your biceps are fully flexed and the barbell is at shoulder height.
Pause for a brief moment and then return the barbell along the same path toward the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Narrow Grip EZ Bar Curls or Cable Curls.

name: Narrow Grip EZ Bar Press
instructions: Lie on a flat bench with an EZ bar loaded to an appropriate weight.
Using a narrow grip lift the bar and hold it straight over your torso with your elbows in. The arms should be perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
Now lower the bar down to your lower chest as you breathe in. Keep the elbows in as you perform this movement.
Using the triceps to push the bar back up, press it back to the starting position by extending the elbows as you exhale.
Repeat.

name: Close-Grip EZ-Bar Curl with Band
instructions: Attach a band to each end of the bar. Take the bar, placing a foot on the middle of the band. Stand upright with a narrow, supinated grip on the EZ bar. The elbows should be close to the torso. This will be your starting position.
While keeping the upper arms in place, flex the elbows to execute the curl. Exhale as the weight is lifted.
Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted and the bar is at shoulder level. Hold the contracted position for a second and squeeze the biceps hard.
Slowly begin to bring the bar back to its starting position as you breathe in.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.

name: Narrow Grip Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lay flat on a bench with a barbell in the rack above you. Take a close grip (6-12 inches between your index fingers) and lift the bar off the rack so that it is directly above your chest. Keep your elbows locked at this point for safety. This will be your starting position.

Whilst inhaling, start to lower the bar by bending at your elbows until the bar gently touches the middle of your chest. Your elbows should remain close together and should brush along the sides of your torso as you go.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the bar back along the same path into the starting position by contracting your triceps and pectoral muscles.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
Caution: You should always have a spotter when attempting this movement. If a spotter is not available you should be conservative with the weight you are using.
Variations: Smith Machine Close Grip Bench Press or Tricep Dips.

name: Barbell Clean Pull
instructions: Grab a barbell with a slightly wider than shoulder width over hand grip. Bend at your knees and hips (more so with your hips than knees), maintaining a straight back and allowing your arms to hang perpendicular to the floor until the bar is just below your knees. Your chest, shoulders and head should be facing forward at this point. This will be your starting position.

Your first movement will be to drive through your heels and quads in order to start elevating the barbell. Your back should remain straight and your arms should still be perpendicular to the floor at this point.

Next comes the acceleration from your pelvis, you need to drive your pelvis forward when the bar gets to your mid thigh. This coupled with the extension through your knee and ankle joints, will propell the barbell upward.
Once you have thrusted your hips forward, it is time to shrug as hard as you can in order to lift the barbell further. Flare your elbows up and outward until your body is at full extension.
After you have achieved full extension you need to bring the bar back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Cleans or Barbell Cleans.

After you have achieved full extension you need to bring the bar back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Cleans or Barbell Cleans.

name: Cleans From Blocks
instructions: With a barbell on boxes or stands of the desired height, take an overhand or hook grip just outside the legs. Lower your hips with the weight focused on the heels, back straight, head facing forward, chest up, with your shoulders just in front of the bar. This will be your starting position.
Begin the first pull by driving through the heels, extending your knees. Your back angle should stay the same, and your arms should remain straight with the elbows pointed out.

Next comes the second pull, the main source of acceleration for the clean. As the bar approaches the mid-thigh position, begin extending through the hips. In a jumping motion, accelerate by extending the hips, knees, and ankles, using speed to move the bar upward. There should be no need to actively pull through the arms to accelerate the weight. At the end of the second pull, the body should be fully extended, leaning slightly back, with the arms still extended.

As full extension is achieved, transition into the receiving position by aggressively shrugging and flexing the arms with the elbows up and out. Aggressively pull yourself down, rotating your elbows under the bar as you do so. Receive the bar in a front squat position, the depth of which is dependent upon the height of the bar at the end of the third pull. The bar should be racked onto the protracted shoulders, lightly touching the throat with the hands relaxed. Continue to descend to the bottom squat position, which will help in the recovery.
Immediately recover by driving through the heels, keeping the torso upright and elbows up. Continue until you have risen to a standing position. Return the weight to the boxes for the next rep.

name: Barbell Clean & Press
instructions: Grab a barbell with a slightly wider than shoulder width over hand grip. Bend at your knees and hips (more so with your hips than knees), maintaining a straight back and allowing your arms to hang perpendicular to the floor until the bar is just below your knees. Your chest, shoulders and head should be facing forward at this point. This will be your starting position.

Your first movement will be to drive through your heels and quads in order to start elevating the barbell. Your back should remain straight and your arms should still be perpendicular to the floor at this point.

Next comes the acceleration from your pelvis, you need to drive your pelvis forward when the bar gets to your mid thigh. This coupled with the extension through your knee and ankle joints, will propell the barbell upward.
Once you have thrusted your hips forward, it is time to shrug as hard as you can in order to lift the barbell further. Flare your elbows up and outward until your body is at full extension.

When at full extension, aggressively squat under the bar and rotate your elbows under and in so that you can take the load on your front deltoids (shoulders) in a front squat position. Continue to squat until you are comfortable to drive upward through your heels, quads and glutes.
Keep driving upward until you are in a standing position.

Once you are in a standing position, bend at your knees to generate power to press the barbell above your head until your elbows are locked out. Tip: Maintaining a straight back throughout is paramount.
Descend the barbell back into the starting position by reversing this movement, making sure to keep the bar close to your body throughout and maintaining good posture.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Barbell Clean & Jerk or Barbell Cleans.

name: Barbell Clean & Jerk
instructions: Grab a barbell with a slightly wider than shoulder width over hand grip. Bend at your knees and hips (more so with your hips than knees), maintaining a straight back and allowing your arms to hang perpendicular to the floor until the bar is just below your knees. Your chest, shoulders and head should be facing forward at this point. This will be your starting position.

Your first movement will be to drive through your heels and quads in order to start elevating the barbell. Your back should remain straight and your arms should still be perpendicular to the floor at this point.

Bend at your knees and hips (more so with your hips than knees), maintaining a straight back and allowing your arms to hang perpendicular to the floor until the bar is just below your knees. Your chest, shoulders and head should be facing forward at this point. This will be your starting position.

Your first movement will be to drive through your heels and quads in order to start elevating the barbell. Your back should remain straight and your arms should still be perpendicular to the floor at this point.

Next comes the acceleration from your pelvis, you need to drive your pelvis forward when the bar gets to your mid thigh. This coupled with the extension through your knee and ankle joints, will propell the barbell upward.
Once you have thrusted your hips forward, it is time to shrug as hard as you can in order to lift the barbell further. Flare your elbows up and outward until your body is at full extension.

When at full extension, aggressively squat under the bar and rotate your elbows under and in so that you can take the load on your front deltoids (shoulders) in a front squat position. Continue to squat until you are comfortable to drive upward through your heels, quads and glutes.
Keep driving upward until you are in a standing position.

Either Drop the bar in a controlled manner if you are on a lifting platform, being careful not to hurt yourself or descend the barbell back into the starting position by reversing this movement, making sure you keep the bar close to your body throughout and maintaining good posture.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Cleans or Kettlebell Cleans.

Variations: Dumbbell Cleans or Kettlebell Cleans.

name: Chain Press
instructions: Begin by connecting the chains to the cable handle attachments. Position yourself on the flat bench in the same position as for a dumbbell press. Your wrists should be pronated and arms perpendicular to the floor. This will be your starting position.
Lower the chains by flexing the elbows, unloading some of the chain onto the floor.
Continue until your elbow forms a 90-degree angle, and then reverse the motion by extending through the elbow to lockout.

en: chest
shoulders
triceps

name: Chain Handle Extension
instructions: You will need two cable handle attachments and a flat bench, as well as chains, for this exercise. Clip the middle of the chains to the handles, and position yourself on the flat bench. Your elbows should be pointing straight up.
Begin by extending through the elbow, keeping your upper arm still, with your wrists pronated.
Pause at the lockout, and reverse the motion to return to the starting position.

en: triceps

You will need two cable handle attachments and a flat bench, as well as chains, for this exercise. Clip the middle of the chains to the handles, and position yourself on the flat bench. Your elbows should be pointing straight up.
Begin by extending through the elbow, keeping your upper arm still, with your wrists pronated.
Pause at the lockout, and reverse the motion to return to the starting position.

name: Chained Deadlifts
instructions: You can attach the chains to the sleeves of the bar, or just drape the middle over the bar so there is a greater weight increase as you lift.

Approach the bar so that it is centered over your feet. You feet should be about hip width apart. Bend at the hip to grip the bar at shoulder width, allowing your shoulder blades to protract. Typically, you would use an overhand grip or an over/under grip on heavier sets. With your feet, and your grip set, take a big breath and then lower your hips and bend the knees until your shins contact the bar.

Look forward with your head, keep your chest up and your back arched, and begin driving through the heels to move the weight upward. After the bar passes the knees, aggressively pull the bar back, pulling your shoulder blades together as you drive your hips forward into the bar.
Lower the bar by bending at the hips and guiding it to the floor.

name: Bradford:Rocky Presses
instructions: Grab a barbell and adopt a shoulder width, over hand grip. Lift the bar above your head so that it is directly above your shoulders. Keep your elbows locked at this point for safety. Maintain a straight back throughout this movement. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, start to lower the bar by bending at your elbows until the bar is below your chin.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the bar back into the starting position by contracting your deltoid muscles (shoulders).
Immediately, inhale and lower the barbell down behind your head by bending and flaring your elbows out wide. Continue to lower the barbell until you are an inch or so away from your shoulders.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the bar back into the starting position by contracting your deltoid muscles (shoulders).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Those with back problems, may find a seated version more comfortable.
Variations: Seated Shoulder Press (Barbell) or Smith Machine Shoulder Press.

name: Bottoms Up Good Morning
instructions: Begin with a bar on a rack at about the same height as your stomach. Bend over underneath the bar and rack the bar across the rear of your shoulders as you would a power squat, not on top of your shoulders. At the proper height, you should be near parallel to the floor when bent over.  Keep your back tight, shoulder blades pinched together, and your knees slightly bent.  Keep your back arched and your cervical spine in proper alignment.
Begin the motion by extending through the hips with your glutes and hamstrings, and you are standing with the weight. Slowly lower the weight back to the pins returning to the starting position.

name: Single Arm Landmine Bent Over Rows
instructions: Load up a barbell with the desired amount of weight, place it into a stirrup or in the corner of the room and take your stance either side of the bar. Grip the bar close to the weight plate(s) with one hand and bend at your knees and waist (more so at your waist) to get your body in position. Your arm should be hanging perpendicular to the floor at this point. This is your starting position. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout.
Exhale and squeeze the muscles in your back to bring the barbell in towards your torso. Bend at the elbow to allow the movement but try not to take any weight through your forearms/bicep.
Once the barbell is as close to your body as it can be or the muscles in your back are as tight as they can be, inhale and return the barbell along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Caution: This exercise may not be suitable for people that suffer from any back pain/injuries.
Variations: Dumbbell Row or Seated One-Arm Cable Pulley Rows.

name: Barbell Anti Gravity Press
instructions: Grab a barbell and lean into an incline bench, making sure your head is off the end. Take hold of the barbell with an overhand grip and hold it close to the bench at shoulder height, parallel to the floor. Have your elbows bent at a 90 degree angle and flared out wide. This is you starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and press the bar away from your shoulders maintaining its height off the floor. Continue to press away from your body until your arms are at full extension (keeping a slight bend at your elbows).
After a brief pause, inhale and return the barbell along the same path back to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Alternating Front Raises or Arnold Dumbbell Press.

name: Biceps Curl
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and adopt a shoulder width stance with your arms hanging perpendicular to the floor, by your sides.This will be your starting position.
To initiate the movement, exhale and curl the dumbbells both up and outward whilst rotating your palms to face the ceiling.
Continue on an upward motion until your biceps are optimally contracted.
After this, inhale and return the dumbbells along the same path back to their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Standing Concentration Curls or Concentration Curls.

name: Landmine Bent Over Two Arm Rows
instructions: Put weight on one of the ends of an Olympic barbell. Make sure that you either place the other end of the barbell in the corner of two walls; or put a heavy object  on the ground so the barbell cannot slide backward.
Bend forward until your torso is as close to parallel with the floor as you can and keep your knees slightly bent.
Now grab the bar with both arms just behind the plates on the side where the weight was placed and put your other hand on your knee. This will be your starting position.

Pull the bar straight up with your elbows in (to maximize back stimulation) until the plates touch your lower chest. Squeeze the back muscles as you lift the weight up and hold for a second at the top of the movement.  Breathe out as you lift the weight. Tip: Use a stirrup or double handle cable attachment by hooking it under the end of the bar.

Slowly lower the bar to the starting position getting a nice stretch on the lats. Tip: Do not let the plates touch the floor.  To ensure the best range of motion, I recommend using small plates (25-lb ones) as opposed to larger plates (like 35-45lb ones).
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: You can perform this exercise using a low pulley or T-bar row machine.

name: Bent Arm Barbell Pullover
instructions: Grab a barbell and lay on a flat bench (face up). Have your arms extended straight up towards the ceiling with your palms facing away from you (at shoulders width). Now Bend your elbows so they are at 90 degrees with the barbell is just above your forehead. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, inhale and slowly bring your elbows towards your ears, lowering the barbell down towards the floor. Tip: Maintain the 90 degree bend at your elbows throughout. The barbell should now be below your head.
After a brief pause, exhale and return the barbell along the same path back to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bent-Arm Dumbbell Pullover or Cable Pullover.

After a brief pause, exhale and return the barbell along the same path back to its starting position.

name: Bench Press with Chains
instructions: Adjust the leader chain, shortening it to the desired length.Place the chains on the sleeves of the bar.  Lying on the bench, get your head beyond the bar if possible. Tuck your feet underneath you and arch your back. Using the bar to help support your weight, lift your shoulder off the bench and retract them, squeezing the shoulder blades together. Use your feet to drive your traps into the bench. Maintain this tight body position throughout the movement. However wide your grip, it should cover the ring on the bar.

Pull the bar out of the rack without protracting your shoulders. Focus on squeezing the bar and trying to pull it apart. Lower the bar to your lower chest or upper stomach. The bar, wrist, and elbow should stay in line at all times.
Pause when the barbell touches your torso, and then drive the bar up with as much force as possible. The elbows should be tucked in until lockout.

Pause when the barbell touches your torso, and then drive the bar up with as much force as possible. The elbows should be tucked in until lockout.

name: Bench Press - With Bands
instructions: Using a flat bench secure a band under the leg of the bench that is nearest to your head.
Once the band is secure, grab it by both handles and lie down on the bench.
Extend your arms so that you are holding the band handles in front of you at shoulder width.
Once at shoulder width, rotate your wrists forward so that the palms of your hands are facing away from you.  This will be your starting position.
Bring down the handles slowly until your elbow forms a 90 degree angle.  Keep full control at all times.

As you breathe out, bring the handles up using your pectoral muscles.  Lock your arms in the contracted position, squeeze your chest, hold for a second and then start coming down slowly.  Tip: It should take at least twice as long to go down than to come up.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions of your training program.
Variations: You can also use a pulley machine, dumbbells or a barbell for this exercise.

name: Belt Squats
instructions: Grab a belt and strap the desired amount of weight to it. Next, place the belt around your waist and make sure it is securely fastened. Adopt a shoulder width stance with your toes pointing slightly outward. This is your starting position. Tip: Throughout the movement you should keep your head up and maintain a straight back.

Descend slowly by bending your knees until your thighs are parallel with the floor. Keep a straight back so that your posture is not compromised. Tip: When at the bottom of the movement your knees should be behind your toes.

Once you have hit the bottom of the movement, pause for a second before pushing your torso back into the starting position through your heels. As you descend inhale and as you ascend, exhale. You should now be at the starting position, ready to go again.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Always be aware of your posture with this exercise as if performed incorrectly, could cause injury.
Variations: Plie Dumbbell Squats or Leg Press (Wide Stance).

name: Standing Barbell Shoulder Press Behind The Head
instructions: Grab a barbell with a wider than shoulder width overhand grip and take a seat on a flat bench. Lift the barbell above your head towards the ceiling so that your elbows are locked out and your palms are facing forward. This is your starting position.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell down behind your head making sure you are nowhere near your neck! Continue until your elbows are at 90 degrees or slightly past.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the barbell back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Smith Machine Overhead Shoulder Press or Standing Dumbbell Press.

name: Barbell Walking Lunges
instructions: Grab and place a barbell across the back of your shoulders. Stand with a straight back, hands placed on the bar for stability in a shoulder width stance. This will be your starting position. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout.

Take a step forward (slightly larger than a normal step) and as you land, drop your body down so that you create two right angles at your knees. Inhale as you do so whilst maintaining a straight torso.
Make sure that you keep your front knee over your heel and not over your toes. Your front shin should now be perpendicular to the floor.
As you exhale, push off the floor with your rear foot to return your body into a standing position.
Alternate legs as you go (as you walk forwards) and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Bodyweight Walking Lunges or Dumbbell Walking Lunges.

name: Barbell Stiff Legged Deadlifts
instructions: Grab a loaded barbell and adopt a standing position with your feet shoulder width apart. Have your arms in front of you but still perpendicular to the floor (overhand grip, palms facing behind you). Slightly bend at your knees and maintain a straight back throughout. This will be your starting position.

Whilst keeping your knees stationary, inhale as you lean forwards from your waist (remember to keep a straight back), keeping the barbell close to you as you go. Keep lowering the barbell towards your feet until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings.

After a brief pause bring your torso upright again by pushing your hips forward. Tip: Remember, this whole movement is performed with 'stiff' legs (slight bend at the knee, but no more) and a straight back throughout. Exhale as you perform this part of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is not an exercise for those with back issues. Avoid jerking motions as this could cause serious injury.
Variation: Dumbbell Stiff Legged Deadlift or Good Mornings.

Grab a loaded barbell and adopt a standing position with your feet shoulder width apart. Have your arms in front of you but still perpendicular to the floor (overhand grip, palms facing behind you). Slightly bend at your knees and maintain a straight back throughout. This will be your starting position.

Whilst keeping your knees stationary, inhale as you lean forwards from your waist (remember to keep a straight back), keeping the barbell close to you as you go. Keep lowering the barbell towards your feet until you feel a stretch in your hamstrings.

After a brief pause bring your torso upright again by pushing your hips forward. Tip: Remember, this whole movement is performed with 'stiff' legs (slight bend at the knee, but no more) and a straight back throughout. Exhale as you perform this part of the movement.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is not an exercise for those with back issues. Avoid jerking motions as this could cause serious injury.
Variation: Dumbbell Stiff Legged Deadlift or Good Mornings.

name: Barbell Step Ups (Alternating)
instructions: Place a barbell across the back of your shoulders and adopt a shoulder width stance in front of a step. The height of the step should be realistic to the ability of the user (make sure you have both hands on the barbell for stability). Maintain a straight back throughout the whole movement so that you minimise risk of injury. This will be your starting position.

Place your right foot on the step/bench. Step up onto the step/bench by pushing through your right heel. As you do so, your hip and knee joints should straighten out. Lift your left leg onto the step/bench at this point. Exhale as you perform this part of the movement.
Step off the step/bench with your left leg by bending at the hip and knee joints of the right leg, inhale as you do so. Return to the starting position by taking your right foot off the step/bench.
Alternate as you go, therefore lead with you left leg on the second repetition.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Step Ups or Barbell Step Ups.

The height of the step should be realistic to the ability of the user (make sure you have both hands on the barbell for stability). Maintain a straight back throughout the whole movement so that you minimise risk of injury. This will be your starting position.

Place your right foot on the step/bench. Step up onto the step/bench by pushing through your right heel. As you do so, your hip and knee joints should straighten out. Lift your left leg onto the step/bench at this point. Exhale as you perform this part of the movement.
Step off the step/bench with your left leg by bending at the hip and knee joints of the right leg, inhale as you do so. Return to the starting position by taking your right foot off the step/bench.
Alternate as you go, therefore lead with you left leg on the second repetition.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Step Ups or Barbell Step Ups.

name: Barbell Step-Ups
instructions: Place a barbell across the back of your shoulders and adopt a shoulder width stance in front of a step. The height of the step should be realistic to the ability of the user (make sure you have both hands on the barbell for stability). Maintain a straight back throughout the whole movement so that you minimise risk of injury. This will be your starting position.

Place your right foot on the step/bench. Step up onto the step/bench by pushing through your right heel. As you do so, your hip and knee joints should straighten out. Lift your left leg onto the step/bench at this point. Exhale as you perform this part of the movement.

Step off the step/bench with your left leg by bending at the hip and knee joints of the right leg, inhale as you do so. Either return to the starting position by taking your right foot off the step/bench, or keep your foot planted.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and then swap legs.
Variations: Dumbbell Step Ups or Barbell Step Ups (Alternating).

name: Barbell Squats To A Bench
instructions: Set the height of the pins in a squat rack so that the barbell can lay across your shoulders comfortably. Next, set the safety bars at the desired height (typically waist high) so that if you fail on a rep, they will take the weight and you will be safe. Place a bench just behind the area you wish to squat in so that it can guide you upon how low to squat. Load up the barbell with the correct amount of weight on it and you are now ready to go.

Step underneath the bar and take the weight across your shoulders whilst maintaining a straight back and a small bend at your knees. Press through your legs to lift the bar and take a small step backwards into the middle of the rack, towards the bench.

Adopt a shoulder width stance with your toes slightly pointing outward just in front of the bench. Maintain a straight back throughout the movement and keep your head up. This will be your starting position.
Inhale and begin to descend by bending at your knees and hips. Your weight should be put predominantly through your heels as you sit back into the movement until your glutes gently touch the bench.

After a brief pause, exhale and return the bar to the starting position along the same path, push through your heels with the force being driven through your quads and glutes to do so. Stand to attention with your legs fully extended, back straight and shoulders relaxed.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is a very complex movement and will require a spotter. If you do not have a spotter, be cautious with your weights. If you suffer from back issues, this exercise may not be suitable.
Variations: Dumbbell Squats or Kettlebell Squats.

name: Static Barbell Side Split Squats
instructions: Stand up straight with a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below your neck), with a wide stance. This is your starting position.

To initiate the movement, inhale and bend your right knee to allow your torso to descend over it. The weight of your body and the bar should now be placed over the right heel and loading your quadricep and glutes with tension. Continue to descend until your knee joint is at 90 degrees. Tip: Your left leg should remain straight and your right foot, flat on the floor.
After a brief pause, exhale and press through your right quadricep and glute to extend your knee. This will return your torso and barbell along the same path back into the starting position.
Alternate as you go and repeat the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Sumo Dumbbell Squats or Leg Press (Wide Stance).

name: Barbell Side Lunges
instructions: Stand up straight with a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below your neck). This will be your starting position.

Take an exaggerated step out to one side and plant your foot with your toes slightly pointing outwards. Maintain a straight back throughout this movement so that you minimise the risk of injury. Inhale, push your hips backward and bend the knee of the foot you have stepped out with in order to lower your weight over that thigh. Once your thigh is parallel with the floor, pause for a brief moment, exhale and push through the heel and thigh from that planted foot.
Throughout this process the leg that remained in the starting position should be straight.
Perform the recommended amount of repetitions and repeat the movement for the opposite leg.
Variation: Leg Press (Wide Stance).

name: Barbell Side Bends
instructions: Stand up straight with a barbell placed on the back of your shoulders (slightly below your neck), with a shoulder width stance. This is your starting position.
To initiate the movement, inhale and lean laterally towards your right side, descending the barbell towards your knee. Continue to descend until you cannot lean any further.
After a brief pause, exhale and flex the obliques and intercostal muscles on your left side and return your torso along the same path back to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: Plate Side Bends or V-Sit Twists (Medicine Ball).

name: Barbell Shrugs Behind The Back
instructions: Grab a barbell behind your back and stand with a shoulder width stance. Your arms should be fully extended ibehind your body with an overhand grip. This will be your starting position.

As you exhale, elevate the barbell by lifting your shoulders and squeezing them in towards your ears. Hold this position for a second. Tip: This movement is generated through the lifting motion created from your shoulders. Therefore your arms should remain relaxed for the most part.
After this pause, return the barbell back to the starting position through the descent of your shoulders.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Shrugs or Smith Machine Shrugs.

After this pause, return the barbell back to the starting position through the descent of your shoulders.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Shrugs or Smith Machine Shrugs.

name: Seated Shoulder Press (Barbell)
instructions: Grab a barbell with a wider than shoulder width overhand grip. Take a seat on a bench that has it's back rest in a 'just off vertical' position and make sure that your back is supported. Lift the barbell above your head towards the ceiling so that your elbows are locked out and your palms are facing forward. This is your starting position.
Inhale and slowly lower the barbell down to your shoulders (infront of your head). Pause for a brief moment when the bar gently touches your shoulders.
Exhale and press the barbell back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Smith Machine Military Press or Standing Military Press.

name: Barbell Seated Calf Raises
instructions: Grab a barbell and take a seat on a flat bench. Have your feet flat on the floor with your knees and ankles at slightly an acute angle (under 90 degrees) and the barbell placed on your thighs. Tip: The further down your thighs towards your knees you place the barbell, the harder the exercise. Keep hold of the barbell for stability as you perform this exercise.

To initiate this movement, exhale and lift your heels off the floor by pressing the ball of your feet into the ground. This will engage and contract your calf muscles. Continue to contract your calves until they are at peak contraction.
After a brief pause, inhale and return your heels along the same path back to their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Calf Raises or Smith Machine Calf Raises.

After a brief pause, inhale and return your heels along the same path back to their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Seated Calf Raises or Smith Machine Calf Raises.

name: Bent Over Barbell Rows
instructions: Load up a barbell with the desired amount of weight and place it on the floor in front of you. Take hold of it with a wider than shoulder width, overhand grip by bending at your knees and waist (more so at your waist) whilst maintaining a straight back. Your arms should be hanging perpendicular to the floor at this point. This is your starting position.
Exhale and squeeze the muscles in your back to bring the barbell in towards your torso. Bend at the elbow to allow the movement but try not to take any weight through your forearms/ biceps.
Once the barbell is as close to your body as it can be or the muscles in your back are at tight as they can be, inhale and return the barbell along the same path back into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This exercise may not be suitable for people that suffer from any back pain/injuries.
Variations: Bent Over Row (Reverse Grip) or Single Arm Leverage Iso Row Machine.

name: Barbell Rollout from Bench
instructions: Grab a barbell and add 5Kgs on each end. Start by placing the bar on the floor just in front of you as you kneel down on a flat bench. Take a shoulder width grip and allow your arms to hang below your shoulders so they are perpendicular to the floor. Tip: Maintain a straight back throughout the movement. This will be your starting position.
Inhale and roll the barbell out away from your body until your body is parallel with the floor (or as far as you can) and your arms are at full reach.

After a brief pause, exhale and pull the bar back along the same path to the starting position. To do so, resist pushing your hips back and instead, contract through your abdominal muscles and pull the bar back in through your arms.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is an advanced movement and should not be attempted if you suffer from any back issues.
Variation: Barbell Ab Rollout or Hanging Leg Raises.

name: Barbell Rear Delt Rows
instructions: Stand up straight, barbell in hand with a wide, overhand (palms facing backward) grip.

Bend at your knees and hips so that your torso is leaning forward toward the floor (lean more so from your hips than your knees). Maintain a straight back and continue to lean until your body is nearly parallel with the floor. Allow your arms to hang perpendicular to the floor. This is your starting position.

Whilst exhaling, flare your elbows out wide and pull the barbell up towards your chest. Pull through the reverse side of your shoulders to elevate the barbell. Keep pulling until the barbell is just off your chest.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the barbell back along the same path to your starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Rear Delt Flyes or Cable Rear Delt Flyes.

name: Barbell Lunges
instructions: Set the height of the pins in a squat rack so that the barbell can lay across your shoulders comfortably. Next, set the safety bars at the desired height (typically waist high) so that if you fail on a rep, they will take the weight and you will be safe. Load up the barbell with the correct amount of weight on it and you are now ready to go.
Step underneath the bar so that it is resting on your shoulders. Take hold of the bar and step back from the rack maintaining a straight back throughout.This will be your starting position.

Inahle and take a slightly exaggerated step forwardand plant your foot firmly on the floor. Whilst maintaining a stragiht back descend your torso towards the floor by bending both knees into a 90 degree position. Tip: Keep your weight central and drop directly downward rather than forward or back, your knees should be at right angles when at the bottom of the movement.
After a brief pause press through your front thigh and heel to push your body back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: If you have any balance issues, you should use an alternative method of adding weight to this exercise. For example, use dumbbells.
Variations: Dumbbell Lunges or Dumbbell Walking Lunges.

name: Landmine Single Arm Jammer
instructions: Grab a barbell and place it into either a stirrup or the corner of the room. Load the barbell up with you desired amount of weight. Next, you will need to adopt a shoulder width stance facing the end of the barbell, bend your knees and hips so that you can then lift the barbell up to your shoulder, keeping hold of it with one hand. Your elbow should be tucked in close to your body at this point. This is your starting position.
To initiate the movement, exhale and press the barbell away from your shoulder by extending your arm out in front of you. Continue pressing until your arm is fully extended.
After a brief pause, inhale and slowly return the barbell along the same path, back into its starting position.
Repeat until you have completed the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Variations: One-Arm Kettlebell Para Press or Dumbbell One-Arm Shoulder Press.

name: Incline Barbell Shoulder Raises
instructions: Grab a barbell with an overhand grip (palms facing away from you) and lay down on an incline bench (around 30 degrees). Next, bring the barbell up to your chest and exhale whilst pressing the bar up towards the ceiling. Your arms should now be extended up above your chest with the bar being parallel to the floor.
Keeping your arms fully extended, exhale and press your shoulders towards the ceiling too, allowing your shoulder blades to separate.
After a brief pause, inhale and slowly return the barbell along the same path, back into its starting position.
Repeat until you have completed the prescribed amount of repetitions.
Variations: Dumbbell Incline Shoulder Raise or Machine Shoulder (Military) Press.

name: Barbell Hip Thrust
instructions: Load up a barbell with your desired amount of weight and set it on the floor. Take a seat on the floor with your legs underneath the bar, your back should be supported by a bench or an elevated step with your shoulder blades being the point of contact.
Roll the bar up and over your legs until it is directly above your hips, bend at your knees and walk your feet in towards your glutes.

Whilst exhaling, squeeze your glutes and drive your heels to lift your hips towards the ceiling. As you do so, lean back over the back support and keep driving upward until your glutes are fully contracted and your body is parallel with the floor.
Inhale and gently relax your glutes to return the barbell back along the same path to the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variation: Barbell Glute Bridge.

name: Barbell Hack Squat
instructions: Load up a barbell and stand in front of it with your calves gently touching the bar.

Whilst maintaining good posture (a straight back), bend at your knees and hips to lower your torso enabling you to take a slightly wider than shoulder width overhand grip. Your hips should remain higher than your knees at all times. This will be the starting position of the exercise.

Grip the bar and initiate the movement by pressing through your heels and quads whilst simultaneously use your glutes and the muscles in your back to bring your torso into a vertical position. Exhale throughout this part of the movement.
After a brief pause, descend the barbell back along the same path and inhale until you are back in the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Be very careful when performing this exercise and if you suffer from any back pain, this may not be suitable movement for you.
Variation: Hex Bar Deadlifts or Cable Deadlifts.

en: quadriceps

Load up a barbell and stand in front of it with your calves gently touching the bar.

Whilst maintaining good posture (a straight back), bend at your knees and hips to lower your torso enabling you to take a slightly wider than shoulder width overhand grip. Your hips should remain higher than your knees at all times. This will be the starting position of the exercise.

Grip the bar and initiate the movement by pressing through your heels and quads whilst simultaneously use your glutes and the muscles in your back to bring your torso into a vertical position. Exhale throughout this part of the movement.
After a brief pause, descend the barbell back along the same path and inhale until you are back in the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Be very careful when performing this exercise and if you suffer from any back pain, this may not be suitable movement for you.
Variation: Hex Bar Deadlifts or Cable Deadlifts.

name: Barbell Guillotine Press
instructions: Lay flat on a bench with a barbell in the rack above you. Take a medium grip (a grip which creates a 90 degree angle at your elbows half way through the movement) and lift the bar off the rack so that it is directly above your chest. Keep your elbows locked at this point for safety. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, start to lower the bar by bending at your elbows until the bar is about 1 inch away from your neck.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the bar back into the starting position by contracting your pectoral muscles.
Repeat the movement for the prescribed amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
Caution: You should always have a spotter when attempting this movement. If a spotter is not available you should be conservative with the weight you are using.
Variations: Smith Machine Incline Chest Press or Incline Dumbbell Press.

name: Barbell Glute Bridge
instructions: Take a seat on the floor with a loaded barbell above your hips. Tip: To minimise your discomfort, place a pad over the bar. Once all is in place, gently place your hands (just wider than shoulder width) on top of the bar and lay down flat on your back. Next, you will need to bend your knees so that the are at a 90 degree angle with your heels firmly planted on the floor. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale, apply pressure into the floor through your upper back/shoulders and drive your hips towards the ceiling. To do so, squeeze your glutes and direct force through your heels. Tip: Stabilise the bar with your overhand grip but take no pressure.
After a brief pause, inhale and return the barbell back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Hip Thrusts or Stability Ball Hip Bridge.

name: Barbell Squats
instructions: Set the height of the pins in a squat rack so that the barbell can lay across your shoulders comfortably. Next, set the safety bars at the desired height (typically waist high) so that if you fail on a rep, they will take the weight and you will be safe. Load up the barbell with the correct amount of weight on it and you are now ready to go.

Step underneath the bar and take the weight across your shoulders whilst maintaining a straight back and a small bend at your knees. Press through your legs to lift the bar and take a small step backwards into the middle of the rack.
Adopt a shoulder width stance with your toes slightly pointing outward. Maintain a straight back throughout the movement and keep your head up. This will be your starting position.

Inhale and begin to descend by bending at your knees and hips. Your weight should be put predominantly through your heels as you sit back into the movement until your thighs are either parallel or just past to the floor.

After a brief pause, exhale and return the bar to the starting position along the same path, push through your heels with the force being driven through your quads and glutes to do so. Stand to attention with your legs fully extended, back straight and shoulders relaxed.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is a very complex movement and will require a spotter. If you do not have a spotter, be cautious with your weights. If you suffer from back issues, this exercise may not be suitable.
Variations: Dumbbell Squats or Kettlebell Squats

name: Barbell Deadlifts
instructions: Load up a barbell and stand behind it with your shins gently touching the bar.

Whilst maintaining good posture (a straight back), bend at your knees and hips to lower your torso enabling you to take a shoulder width overhand grip. This grip position should be just outside of your knees and your hips should remain higher than your knees. This will be the starting position of the exercise. Tip: You can adopt an alternate grip (one overhand and one underhand) if it is more comfortable for you.

Grip the bar and initiate the movement by pressing through your heels and quads whilst simultaneously use the muscles in your back to bring your torso into a vertical position. Throughout the movement keep the bar close to your body, maintain strict form (straight back) and exhale. Once you are in a standing position you should push your chest out by pulling your shoulder blades together.
After a brief pause, descend the barbell back along the same path and inhale until you are back in the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Be very careful when performing this exercise and if you suffer from any back pain, this may not be suitable movement for you.
Variation: Rack Pulls or Dumbbell Bent Over Rows.

name: Lying Incline Barbell Curls
instructions: Grab a barbell and take a seat on an incline bench (around 30-45 degrees). Take hold of the bar with an underhand grip and pin your elbows in to your torso. Allow the bar to rest on your thighs at this point. This will be your starting position.

While holding the upper arm stationary, curl the barbell up towards you shoulders by contracting your biceps as you exhale. Continue the movement until your biceps are fully contracted. Hold the contracted position for a second as you squeeze your bicep. Tip: Only the forearms should move.
Inhale and return the barbell along the same path back to its starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Incline Dumbbell Curls or Cable Preacher Curls.

en: biceps

name: Standing Barbell Curls
instructions: Grab a barbell with a shoulder width grip (palms facing forward), and allow your arms the hang perpendicular to the floor. Adopt a standing position with a shoulder width stance and a straight back. Keep your elbows pinned in close to your torso for the duration of the exercise. This will be your starting position.
Whilst keeping your upper arms stationary, curl the barbell upward toward your shoulders by flexing your biceps. Exhale as you do so and remember, only your forearms should move.
Continue in this fashion until your biceps are fully flexed and the barbell is at shoulder height.
Pause for a brief moment and then return the barbell along the same path toward the starting position as you inhale.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Wide Grip EZ Bar Curls or Cable Curls.

name: Barbell Bench Press
instructions: Lay flat on a bench with a barbell in the rack above you. Take a medium grip (a grip which creates a 90 degree angle at your elbows half way through the movement) and lift the bar off the rack so that it is directly above your chest. Keep your elbows locked at this point for safety. This will be your starting position.
Whilst inhaling, start to lower the bar by bending at your elbows until the bar gently touches the middle of your chest.
After a brief pause, exhale and press the bar back into the starting position by contracting your pectoral muscles.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
When you are done, place the bar back in the rack.
Caution: You should always have a spotter when attempting this movement. If a spotter is not available you should be conservative with the weight you are using.
Variations: Smith Machine Chest Press or Flat Dumbbell Chest Press.

name: Standing Barbell Rollout
instructions: Grab a barbell and add 5Kgs on each end. Start by putting the bar on the floor just in front of your shins and adopt a shoulder width grip. Maintain a straight back with a gentle bend at your knees throughout this movement. This will be your starting position.
Inhale and roll the barbell out away from your body until you reach a 'press up' position. Tip: Always keep your arms perpendicular to the floor as this will help to keep tension on the abdominals.
After a brief pause, exhale and pull the bar back along the same path to the starting position. To do so, push your hips back and contract through your abdominal muscles.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: This is an advanced movement and should not be attempted if you suffer from any back issues.
Variation: Barbell Ab Rollout (From Knees), Exercise Ball Pull-In or Hanging Leg Raises.

name: Dumbbell Swings
instructions: Grab a Dumbbell with your right hand and adopt a slightly wider than shoulder width stance. Take hold of the dumbbell with a neutral grip and stand upright with the weight in front of you. This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, inhale and bend at your knees and hips so that your thighs are parallel to the floor. At the same time bring the dumbbell between your legs and push your hand towards the floor to emphasise the power generated in your legs.

Immediately after doing this, exhale and use this power to drive through your heels, thighs and hips to pull the dumbbell to head height with your arm remaining at full extension however rotating the dumbbell so you now have an overhand grip (palm facing the floor).
Inhale and return the dumbbell along the same path back between your legs as you squat into the next repetition.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions and swap sides.
Variations: One-Arm Kettlebell Swings or Cleans.

name: Zottman Preacher Curls
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and take a seat on a preacher curl machine. Place your upper arms over the pad of the machine with your palms facing your shoulders. Start with your biceps fully contracted and the dumbbells close to your shoulders. This will be your starting position.
As you inhale, slowly lower the dumbbells until your upper arms are extended and your biceps are fully stretched.
Exhale and contract your biceps to lift the dumbbells back up to the starting position.
Squeeze your biceps for a second at the contracted position and repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Cable Curls or EZ Bar Bicep Curls.

en: biceps

name: Tate Press
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and lay down on a bench, bringing the dumbbells beside your chest. Keep your elbows at a 90-degree angle and your forearms perpendicular to the floor. Once here, keep your palms facing away from you, exhale and press the dumbbells towards the ceiling.
To initiate the movement, inhale and tilt the dumbbell in towards your chest. Allow your elbows to flare out wide. This will load your pectoral and tricep muscles.
Once the dumbbells are either just off your chest or gently touching your pectoral muscles exhale and press the dumbbells back along the same path into the starting position.
Repeat the movement for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Always have a spotter with you to help you throughout the movement and to help unload the dumbbells after completion.
Variations: EZ-Bar Skullcrusher or Cable Skull Crushers.

name: Sumo Dumbbell Squats
instructions: Grab a dumbbell by one of its ends in both hands and stand up straight. Maintain this straight posture throughout the movement and your arms remain passive and extended. Adopt a wide stance with your toes slightly facing outwards. This will be your starting position.

Inhale as you descend by bending at your knees. Remember to keep your knees behind your toes and put most of your weight through your heels. The dumbbell should now be just off the floor with your arms being between your legs.
After a brief pause, Push through your heels and thighs to bring your torso back up into the starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Caution: Make sure to maintain posture as failure to do so could result in injury.
Variations: Barbell Sumo Deadlifts or Kettlebell Goblet Squats.

name: Dumbbell Pull Overs
instructions: Place a dumbbell standing up on a flat bench.

Ensuring that the dumbbell stays securely placed at the top of the bench, lie perpendicular to the bench (torso across it as in forming a cross) with only your shoulders lying on the surface.  Hips should be below the bench and legs bent with feet firmly on the floor.  The head will be off the bench as well.

Grasp the dumbbell with both hands and hold it straight over your chest at arms length.  Both palms should be pressing against the underside one of the sides of the dumbbell.  This will be your starting position. Caution: Always ensure that the dumbbell used for this exercise is secure.  Using a dumbbell with loose plates can result in the dumbbell falling apart and falling on your face.
While keeping your arms straight, lower the weight slowly in an arc behind your head while breathing in until you feel a stretch on the chest.
At that point, bring the dumbbell back to the starting position using the arc through which the weight was lowered and exhale as you perform this movement.
Hold the weight on the initial position for a second and repeat the motion for the prescribed number of repetitions.

Caution: If you are new to this movement, have a spotter hand you the weight instead.  If not, please ensure that the dumbbell does not fall on you as you arrange your torso to perform the exercise on the bench.
Also, as I already mentioned, ensure that the dumbbell used is in perfect working condition.  Old dumbbells in need of welding should never be used to perform this exercise.
Variations: Barbell Pullovers or Straight Arm Pulldowns.

name: Standing Palms-In Dumbbell Press
instructions: Grab a dumbbell in each hand and adopt a shoulder width stance. Bring the dumbbells up to your shoulders with an underhand grip (palms facing your torso). This will be your starting position.

To initiate the movement, exhale and press the dumbbells upward, towards the ceiling. Continue to press the dumbbells upward until the muscles in your shoulders are at optimal contraction or your arms are at full reach (elbow slightly bent).
After a brief pause, inhale and return the dumbbells back along the same path to their starting position.
Repeat for the recommended amount of repetitions.
Variations: Kettlebell Arnold Press or Seated Cable Shoulder Press.
